title: The AI Power Play Wall Street Is Still Sleeping On
subtitle: Vistra just closed a nuclear deal with Meta, added 4,000+ MW of zero-carbon generation, and trades at a discount to every other IPP with an AI story. Here's why that won't last.
summary: Vistra Corp (VST) is the overlooked middle ground in the AI power race — nuclear baseload from the Energy Harbor acquisition, a massive gas fleet for peaking, 5 million retail customers as a natural hedge, and a Meta PPA that signals hyperscaler demand for 24/7 carbon-free power. With a forward P/E of ~14.4x versus CEG at 18.6x and a PEG of 0.45, the market is pricing Vistra like a regulated utility when it's anything but.

image.caption: Vistra Corp is quietly becoming one of the most strategically positioned independent power producers in the AI era.

links.label: The Meta-Vistra Deal: A Closer Look — ANS →

bodyHtml:
Here's a question: when you think about the companies powering the AI revolution, who comes to mind? Nvidia, obviously. Maybe the hyperscalers — Microsoft, Amazon, Google. The chip guys, the cloud guys, the networking guys. But here's who probably doesn't come to mind: a Texas-based power company you've maybe heard of once, with 44 gigawatts of generation, 5 million retail customers, and a brand-new nuclear deal with Meta that nobody on Wall Street has fully priced in yet.

That company is Vistra Corp. And it might be the most interesting AI infrastructure bet you're not paying attention to.

Let's back up. Vistra is an integrated retail electricity company and independent power producer — a fancy way of saying they both generate power and sell it directly to homes and businesses. They operate across ERCOT, PJM, ISO-NE, NYISO, MISO. Their fleet is a mix of nuclear, natural gas, coal, solar, and battery storage. But here's what changed the story: the Energy Harbor acquisition closed in 2024, a roughly $6.3 billion deal that added over 4,000 megawatts of nuclear generation to Vistra's portfolio. Overnight, Vistra went from a gas-and-coal operator to one of the largest nuclear fleet owners in the country.

And nuclear, as you probably know, is the holy grail for AI data centers right now. Hyperscalers need 24/7 carbon-free power. They can't get it from solar alone. They can't get it from wind alone. They need baseload zero-carbon generation that runs every hour of every day — and nuclear is the only scalable answer at the moment. That's why Constellation (CEG) landed a headline-grabbing PPA with Microsoft. That's why Talen Energy's Susquehanna nuclear site got the AWS data center deal. And that's why Meta came knocking on Vistra's door.

The Meta-Vistra deal, reported by the American Nuclear Society back in January, is a power purchase agreement that puts Vistra's nuclear fleet at the center of Meta's AI infrastructure plans. Think about what that signals. Meta could have gone to anyone — they chose Vistra. They chose the company that, until recently, the market treated as a boring regulated utility with some coal plants and a retail business. That perception is now two years out of date.

Here's where the disconnect gets really interesting. Vistra trades at roughly 14.4 times forward earnings. Constellation, its closest nuclear competitor, trades at 18.6 times. That's a meaningful discount — and it's even harder to justify when you look at Vistra's PEG ratio of 0.45. Anything under 1.0 traditionally signals an undervalued growth story. At 0.45, the market is basically saying Vistra's growth is already baked in, or it's not real. Both of those takes look wrong from here.

The financials back it up. Vistra is doing nearly $20 billion in revenue, growing over 40% year-over-year when you factor in the Energy Harbor contribution. Operating margins sit around 27%. Return on equity is pushing 43%. Those aren't utility numbers — those are industrial-growth numbers dressed up in a boring sector. Sure, free cash flow is negative right now, but that's because Vistra is spending roughly $2.8 billion annually on capex — building new gas plants, adding battery storage, expanding solar. They're investing in the capacity that the AI buildout demands. That's not a red flag. That's a sign of a company that sees the opportunity and is going after it.

And the opportunity is real. PJM capacity auction prices have been surging as coal retirements tighten supply. Vistra's 5,500-megawatt Cogentrix gas plants acquisition gives them even more flexibility to capture those peaks. The company is adding 4.5 gigawatts of new capacity — a mix of gas, solar, and battery storage — announced just this May. That's a massive buildout for a company the market still wants to value like it's just another Texas electricity retailer.

Eighteen analysts cover VST. Fifteen say buy. Four say strong buy. One sell. The consensus rating sits at 1.4 — firmly in buy territory. And the company is putting its money where its mouth is, buying back over a billion dollars in stock annually while maintaining a tiny but growing dividend. That's a management team that believes in the thesis.

So here's the thing. You've got three big AI power narratives right now. Constellation, with the Microsoft deal and the biggest nuclear fleet, trading at premium multiples. Talen, with the AWS on-campus data center but mired in FERC drama and regulatory uncertainty. And then you've got Vistra — nuclear from the Energy Harbor deal, gas for peaking, retail for the natural hedge, a Meta deal in the bag, and a valuation that still hasn't caught up. It's the middle path. Less headline risk than Talen. More upside potential than Constellation. And nobody's talking about it.

That usually doesn't last.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in $VST. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
